md: A **system of equations** is two or more equations sharing the same variables. A *solution* is a set of values that makes **every equation true at the same time**.
For two linear equations in two variables, the geometric picture is gorgeous: each equation is a line, and the solution is **wherever the lines cross**.
**1.** Graph both equations on the same coordinate plane.
**3.** Read off its coordinates — that's the solution.
**4.** Check by plugging the values into both original equations.
Adjust the two lines below. The red dot marks where they meet — that's the system's solution. Slide one line until the dot lands on a nice integer point and watch the readout follow.
Two lines in the plane can interact in only three ways:

blocks.title: One solution
blocks.body: **Lines have different slopes.** They cross at exactly one point. The system has a single $(x, y)$ solution.

blocks.title: No solution
blocks.body: **Lines are parallel** (same slope, different y-intercepts). They never meet. The system has *no values* that satisfy both.

blocks.title: Infinitely many solutions
blocks.body: **The two equations describe the same line** (same slope and y-intercept). Every point on the line satisfies both equations.

md: Both are already in slope-intercept form. The first has slope $2$ and y-intercept $-1$; the second has slope $-1$ and y-intercept $5$. Graph them — they cross at $(2, 3)$.
**Check.** Plug into the first: $y = 2(2) - 1 = 3$ ✓. Plug into the second: $y = -(2) + 5 = 3$ ✓.

blocks.title: Limitations of graphing
blocks.body: Graphing is great for *understanding* the geometry, but it's not always precise — non-integer intersections like $(2.7, -1.3)$ are hard to read off a graph. For exact answers, use **substitution** or **elimination** (the next two subtopics).

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Graph both equations; the intersection is the solution. Three cases: one point (different slopes), parallel (no solution), same line (infinitely many). Always check your answer in both equations.
